metadata.title: Build the Output Gallery: 10–15 Real Examples
metadata.playlist: Make It Smart — Scale Your Madison Intelligence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 4 brief — Scale Your Thing & Add Intelligence

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 4 of 7. One output proves the workflow can run once. Ten to fifteen outputs prove it is reliable. The gallery is the difference between a demo and a product. Each entry in the gallery has four things: what was produced, where it went, a screenshot or file link, and an honest quality check. The quality check is what separates a gallery from a dump — it answers: does this output actually work? Any issues? Today you run the workflow enough times to fill the gallery and fill the entry template for each one. Let's go.

props.segment: Build the Output Gallery: 10–15 Real Examples
props.command: Generate 10–15 real outputs. Document each with: what produced · where it went · proof · quality check.
props.runningText: one output proves it runs; ten prove it's reliable…
output: Template per entry: what produced · file path or ID · screenshot · quality check
Quality check: does this output actually work? Any issues?
Consistency across the set is the point — not variety

beats.narration_text: Every gallery entry has the same four fields. One: what was produced. A one-sentence description of this specific output — not 'an HTML report' in the abstract, but 'an HTML report on AI regulation trends for the week of July 14, with 23 records classified.' The more specific, the more useful. Two: where it went. The file path on your computer, or the post ID if you used a test account. Something that uniquely identifies this artifact from the others. Three: proof. A screenshot of the artifact open, cropped to show the content — not just the file in Finder, but the file open and readable. Four: quality check. One sentence answering two questions: does this output work as intended, and are there any visible issues — truncated text, missing fields, wrong format, or AI output that does not make sense? A quality check that says 'yes, no issues' on all fifteen entries is a quality check that was not actually performed. The honest ones mention at least one issue.

artifactLines: 1. What produced: specific description — not 'an HTML report' but the specific content + record count.
2. Where it went: file path on disk or post ID — unique identifier for this artifact.
3. Proof: screenshot of the artifact open and readable — not the file in Finder.
4. Quality check: does it work? Any issues (truncated text, missing fields, odd AI output)?
Rule: 'yes, no issues' on all 15 entries = quality check not performed. Honest ones note issues.

beats.narration_text: Ten to fifteen outputs means running the workflow on ten to fifteen different input records. Not running the same record fifteen times — fifteen different records producing fifteen different outputs. Each run should produce a distinct artifact: a different report, a different classification result, a different row in the CSV. If your workflow processes one record at a time, run it fifteen times on fifteen different input records from your A3 data file. If your workflow processes records in batches, make sure the gallery documents fifteen distinct entries from the batch output — not fifteen rows from the same report. The point is to demonstrate consistent performance across different inputs. That is what reliability means: the same workflow producing usable output from record after record, not just from the one example you built it for.

artifactLines: 10–15 different input records → 10–15 distinct output artifacts. Not the same record 15 times.
One-at-a-time workflow: run it 15 times on 15 different records from the A3 data file.
Batch workflow: document 15 distinct entries from the batch output — not 15 rows from one report.
The point: consistent performance across different inputs = reliability.
Same workflow, different inputs, usable output each time — that's what you're proving.
props.sparkLine: 15 different inputs. 15 distinct outputs. That's reliability.

beats.narration_text: Three traps in the gallery. Trap one: fifteen near-identical dumps. If every entry in your gallery is an HTML report with the same structure, the same approximate content, and no quality notes, the gallery looks like you ran the same record fifteen times and took fifteen screenshots. Each entry needs enough distinct content to show it came from a different input record. The title and date fields alone are sufficient distinguishers. Trap two: no quality check. The rubric requires an honest quality check for each entry. 'Good' or 'no issues' is not a quality check — it is a signal that you did not read the output. A real quality check mentions at least one observation per entry: whether the AI classification seems accurate, whether the output format is consistent, whether any field is missing or truncated. Trap three: claiming success with no proof. An entry that says 'produced an HTML report' but has no screenshot or file path does not count. The artifact must be proven to exist.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Identical dumps: 15 entries with no distinguishing content = same record 15 times.
Fix: each entry needs title/date/record-specific content visible in the screenshot.
Trap 2 — No quality check: 'Good' on all 15 = not checked. Note at least one observation per entry.
Trap 3 — No proof: 'produced a report' without screenshot or file path = artifact does not exist.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Build the Output Gallery. Ten to fifteen entries, each from a different input record, each with four fields: what was produced, where it went, proof it exists, and an honest quality check. The quality check must say something specific about each output — not 'good' across all fifteen. Run the workflow on different input records from the A3 data file. Document each run as it completes — do not try to fill in fifteen entries from memory after the fact. A gallery of identical-looking entries with no quality notes and no file paths tells the grader you generated one output and duplicated the entry. Episode 5 is the stress test: you will run fifty requests on test data and report what breaks.

props.artifactTitle: Build the Output Gallery: 10–15 Real Examples
artifactLines: 10–15 entries from different input records — not the same record repeated.
Template: what produced · file path or ID · proof screenshot · quality check.
Quality check: specific observation per entry. 'Good' across all = not checked.
Fill entries as you run — not from memory after the fact.
Identical entries with no file paths = one output with duplicated rows.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude to generate your gallery entry template and fill the first one. The prompt reads: I am building a gallery of outputs for an n8n workflow. The workflow takes a record with these fields: [list your AI output fields from Episode 1]. It produces this type of output: [one sentence describing your output format — HTML report, CSV row, chart, etc.]. Write the gallery entry template for this project — all four fields with example text showing what a good entry looks like for my specific output type. Then fill the first entry using this sample output: [paste one real output from a workflow run]. Mark any field in the quality check where the output needs improvement. That prompt gives you the template and fills the first entry as an example. Fill the remaining fourteen entries yourself, one at a time, after each workflow run. The quality check is the field most people skip — write it for every entry before moving to the next run.

props.segment: Build the Output Gallery: 10–15 Real Examples
props.command: My AI output fields: [list]. Output type: [one sentence]. Write the gallery entry template for this project — all 4 fields with example text. Then fill the first entry using this real output: [paste one workflow output]. Mark the quality check field where the output needs improvement.
props.runningText: fill quality check for every entry before the next run — it's the field people skip…

beats.narration_text: Build the Output Gallery: 10–15 Real Examples. Liam, in for Bear.
